senses.definition: (of information) from an accurate or valid source

examples.x: I heard it from the horse's mouth that the company is planning a major expansion next year.
You can trust this news; it came straight from the horse's mouth.
I got the inside scoop from the CEO himself ‚Äď it's from the horse's mouth.
When it comes to family matters, I prefer to hear it from the horse's mouth, not through gossip.
She learned about the promotion directly from her manager, so it's information from the horse's mouth.

word_list.pronunciation: blňąo ä √į…ô wňą…™s…ôl ňĆ…Ďňźn ňĆ…õsbňąiňź slňą√¶ É ňĆ…õstňĆiňźňąe…™t É

senses.definition: to inform the authorities about something secret or the breach of legalities, particularly because one is morally obliged to do so

examples.x: She decided to blow the whistle on the fraudulent accounting practices at the company.
The brave whistleblower blew the whistle on the government's illegal surveillance program.
Employees are encouraged to blow the whistle on any instances of harassment in the workplace.
He blew the whistle on the corrupt officials who were embezzling public funds.

senses.definition: to hear something that was passed from one person to another, like a rumor or gossip

examples.x: We keep hearing it on the grapevine that the two co-stars of that movie are getting married, but they are not saying anything.
Within professional football itself, other managers do get to hear things on the grapevine.

senses.definition: fully informed about or actively participating in something, such as a discussion or a process

examples.x: I'll make sure you're in the loop regarding any updates on the project.
She's always in the loop when it comes to the latest technology developments.
Being in the loop allows you to have a say in the decision-making process.

senses.definition: to stay informed about or completely in control of a situation and how it develops
to provide someone with sufficient and relevant information regarding someone or something

examples.x: I'll keep you posted on the progress of the project.
Please keep me posted if there are any changes to the schedule.
Don't worry; I'll keep you posted on the latest news regarding the event.
She asked her assistant to keep her posted on any emails that require immediate attention.

senses.definition: to provide someone with information they require to handle or understand a situation

examples.x: Before we proceed with the project, let's put everyone in the picture about our goals and expectations.
I need to put you in the picture regarding the changes in the company's policies.
Could you please put me in the picture regarding the agenda for the upcoming meeting?

senses.definition: (of news, information, or rumors) to quickly become known by the majority

examples.x: The news of the scandal spread like wildfire throughout the company.
Word of the upcoming sale spread like wildfire among bargain hunters.
The rumor about their breakup spread like wildfire, causing quite a buzz.
In the age of the internet, information can spread like wildfire, both true and false.
The false reports were spreading like wildfire before they were debunked.

senses.definition: with an extensive knowledge concerning current topics and events

examples.x: She's really up on the latest technology trends.
John is up on current events; he reads the news every morning.
To succeed in this field, you need to be up on the latest research.
Being up on industry developments is crucial for our business.

senses.definition: (of information)  received without any intervention of other people or agencies

examples.x: She got the information first hand from the witness to the accident.
I wanted to hear the news first hand from the source, so I attended the press conference.

word_list.pronunciation: ba…™ őł…Ļuňź √į…ô wňą…úňźd  Ćv mňąa äőł

senses.definition: by telling others rather than writing or showing it to them

examples.x: Most of our customers hear about us through the word of mouth.
The book became a worldwide best-seller, largely by the word of mouth.
The legend of the hidden treasure was passed down by word of mouth for generations.
We heard about the event through word of mouth, as there was no official announcement.
The success of the restaurant spread by word of mouth, thanks to satisfied customers.

senses.definition: a warning or notification provided in advance to inform someone about a situation, often to prepare them for what is coming
an anonymous or unidentified source of information

examples.x: I heard from a little bird that there's a surprise party planned for you this weekend.
A little bird told me you have a secret admirer.
I can't reveal my sources, but let's just say a little bird told me you're in for a big surprise tomorrow.
If it wasn't you, then I guess a little bird told him.

senses.definition: used to indicate that someone believes others are talking about them even when they are absent
a situation where a message gets passed from one person to another, but it often gets changed along the way
(of a piece of information or news) recently received

examples.x: The breaking news is hot off the press, so we'll provide updates as we get more information.
This report is hot off the press, so you're among the first to see the findings.
The magazine features an interview with the celebrity that's hot off the press.

senses.definition: not having recent information regarding a certain thing, particularly an event

examples.x: He's been out of touch with the music scene for years and doesn't know any of the current bands.
Her views on technology are so outdated; she's completely out of touch with the latest innovations.
"The older generation sometimes feels out of touch with the interests and concerns of younger people.
The professor's outdated lectures show that he is completely out of touch.
The politician's remarks demonstrate that he is out of touch with the needs and struggles of everyday people.

senses.definition: a collection of documents that provide written or recorded version of someone's activities

examples.x: The detective followed the suspect's paper trail to uncover the financial irregularities.
A well-maintained paper trail is essential for demonstrating compliance with regulatory requirements.
The company kept a meticulous paper trail of all its business transactions.
In the absence of a paper trail, it can be challenging to reconstruct what happened.
The auditor reviewed the paper trail of expenses to identify any discrepancies.

senses.definition: an attempt made to find out the facts about something via collecting a lot of information, often secretly

examples.x: The prosecutor accused the defense attorney of going on a fishing expedition by requesting extensive records unrelated to the case.
The auditor's request for all financial records from the past decade seemed like a fishing expedition rather than a focused review.
It's important to have a legitimate reason for conducting an investigation and not simply embark on a fishing expedition.
The journalist's article felt like a fishing expedition, as it made vague allegations without concrete evidence.
The company's internal review was more of a fishing expedition than a targeted analysis of the problem.
